unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 9 — the final unit of Year 4! This unit is all about places, people, and plans. You will learn the words we use to talk about travelling: the airport, the station, the museum, the capital city, and much more. You will meet the people we find in busy places, like the customer at a shop and the workers in an office. Most of all, you will learn how to make plans — how to invite a friend, how to say what you are going to do, and how to give clear directions. By the end, you will be able to plan a whole trip in English, just like Selma and Isaac do in this unit's story when they explore the city of Mombasa.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a small group of place words each day.
Work through the Grammar file. There are eight short lessons — do one or two at a time.
Read the Story file, “The Day We Got Lost in Mombasa”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four skills below in order: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to check what you have learned this year.

visual.alt: Learners planning routes and describing places and people

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name many kinds of places — the airport, station, museum, mall, and more.
Talk about the people and movement in busy places, using words like customer and arrive.
Invite someone politely using would like to.
Talk about your future plans using going to and the present continuous.
Compare how people do things using comparative and superlative adverbs (faster, most carefully).
Give clear instructions and directions using imperatives (Take the elevator. Turn left.).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Check what someone thinks using question tags (You're coming, aren't you?).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Plan and describe a trip around your capital city in writing and speaking.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

readings.title: A Trip to the Capital
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: Last month, my family took a trip to the capital city. First, we went to the big station and waited for the train. When the train arrived, we found seats near the window and watched the horizon rush past. In the city there was so much to see! We visited a museum full of old maps and tools. Then we walked through a busy neighbourhood, past shops, offices, and a tall building with a glass elevator. Tourism brings many people to the capital, so the streets were full. At the end of the day, we ate at a small restaurant near the entrance of the market. I was tired, but I would like to go again next year.

readings.title: Living Near the Equator
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: Kenya sits close to the equator, the imaginary line around the middle of the Earth. Near the equator, the sun is very strong and the days are warm all year. People still build, travel, and work hard here. Trains run on the railroad, ships arrive at the coast, and factories make goods for the whole nation. Whether you live in a small neighbourhood or a big capital, there is always a place to go and a plan to make.

readings.title: Making a Plan (listening script)
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Samira: “We're going to explore the capital on Saturday, aren't we?”
Isaac: “Yes! First we are going to the museum. Then we would like to try the new restaurant near the station. Would you like to come?”
Samira: “I'd love to! Let's arrive early so we have more time. I'm so excited!”
Isaac: “Me too! I'll bring the map.”

readings.title: Directions at the Mall (listening script)
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: “Welcome to the mall! To find the bookshop, take the elevator to the second floor. Turn left and walk down the long hallway. The shop is near the entrance, next to the juice stand. Please walk carefully — the floor can be slippery!”

readings.title: The Day We Got Lost in Mombasa
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
“We're going to the capital, aren't we?” asked Selma, holding her small travel bag.
“Not today,” said her uncle with a smile. “We're visiting Mombasa! It's a special city by the ocean. There's a museum, a station, and even a big restaurant near the sea.”
Selma and her cousin Isaac were excited. It was their first time travelling without their parents. Uncle Adam was in charge.
They arrived at the station earlier than planned. “Let's buy some juice while we wait,” said Isaac.
At the juice stand, the customer in front of them bought three bottles. “Take your time,” said the seller kindly.
Musa whispered, “This place is so busy. The neighbourhood feels different from ours.”
Selma nodded. “It's bigger. More tourism, maybe. Look at all the visitors!”
When their train came, they found seats near the window. “I want to see the horizon,” said Selma. “I love how the sea meets the sky.”
In Mombasa, the air was hotter, and the streets were full of colour. “First stop,” said Uncle Adam, “the museum.”
Inside, they saw old tools, maps, and animal bones. A guide pointed to the wall. “This was found near the equator,” he explained. “The heat there is strong, but people still build and work hard.”
Selma and Isaac took photos. “Now, let's go to the mall before lunch,” said Uncle Adam.
At the mall, they looked for gifts and rode the elevator for fun. “We went up to the fourth floor faster than I thought!” said Isaac.
After that, they stopped at a restaurant. “Would you like to try local fish, or chicken and rice?” asked Uncle Adam.
“I'd like the chicken,” said Isaac. “Me too!” added Selma.
“You two always agree,” he said, laughing.
“I feel hungry already,” said Selma.
The waiter walked them to their table. “The entrance is beautiful,” said Selma, looking at the painted doors.
After lunch, Uncle Adam had a quick call at the office where he once worked. “Wait by the shop near the hallway, okay?”
“Okay,” said Selma. “We'll stay carefully nearby.”
But the shop was full of wonderful souvenirs — little aeroplanes for the airport, capital city keychains, and even a tiny model railroad!
Musa's eyes grew wide. “Let's just look at that shop across the street.”
“But Uncle said to wait,” Selma warned.
“It won't take long,” said Isaac.
They crossed the road and looked around. But when they turned back, the building looked different. The entrance had a blue sign now, not a green one.
“This isn't the place!” said Selma, her heart beating fast. “Where's the hallway? Where's the office?”
They were lost.
Selma took a slow breath. “Let's not panic. Let's ask someone at the rental stand,” she said.
A kind woman there smiled. “I saw your uncle go that way. Take the elevator up. His office is on the second floor.”
They thanked her and followed her directions. Carefully, they stepped into the elevator and pressed the button.
When the door opened, they saw Uncle Adam at the end of the hallway.
“You're going to give me grey hair!” he said, but then he smiled. “You're safe — that's what matters most.”
“We were just curious,” said Isaac, looking down.
“Curiosity is good,” Uncle replied gently. “But you must stay careful in busy places, and keep to the plan.”
Back at the train station, they waited with cold drinks.
“We saw so many places today,” said Selma. “The factories, the market, even the coast.”
“I liked the museum the best,” said Isaac. “It teaches more than the mall does.”
Uncle nodded. “That's the difference between shopping and learning.”
“Would you like to travel again?” he asked.
“Yes!” they shouted together.
“Maybe next time we'll see the airport,” said Selma.
“Or climb a hill near the equator,” added Isaac.
They laughed, feeling tired but happy. As the train pulled away, Selma looked out at the setting sun. “I can still see the ocean,” she said softly, “far away on the horizon.”

grammar.title: Would like to
grammar.explanation: “Would like to” is a polite way to say what you want, or to invite someone to do something. It is a kinder, softer way of saying “I want to”. We use it a lot when we are being friendly and respectful.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use: would like to + the base verb (the plain form of the action).
“I would like to visit the museum.”
“She would like to play football.”
To invite someone: “Would you like to come with us?”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “I would like visit the park.”  ✗
Fix: Don't forget the little word to: “I would like to visit the park.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think: “would like to + do”. The word “to” builds a tiny bridge to the action.
grammar.practice: Complete each sentence with “would like to” and the verb in brackets. | She ______ (visit) her grandparents this weekend. | They ______ (play) football after school. | ______ you ______ (join) us for lunch?

grammar.title: Going to (Future Plans)
grammar.explanation: We use “going to” to talk about plans — things we have already decided to do in the future. It tells people what we intend to do.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use: subject + am/is/are + going to + base verb.
I → am:  “I am going to visit my aunt.”
He / She / It → is:  “She is going to join the team.”
You / We / They → are:  “We are going to explore the capital.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “I going to play.”  ✗
Fix: Never forget the “to be” word: “I am going to play.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: “Going to” is like a road sign pointing to the future. First check: “Am I planning to do it?” If yes, use “going to”.
grammar.practice: Complete each sentence with the correct form of “going to”. | They ______ (visit) the museum next week. | I ______ (have) a party on Saturday. | She ______ (read) a new book this month.

grammar.title: Comparative and Superlative Adverbs
grammar.explanation: Adverbs tell us HOW something is done (quickly, carefully). We compare them to show that one action is done more, or the most. This helps us describe differences clearly.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Short adverbs (one syllable): add -er and -est:  fast → faster → fastest.
Adverbs ending in -ly: use more and most:  carefully → more carefully → most carefully.
Irregular: well → better → best.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “He arrived more fast than me.”  ✗
Fix: “fast” is short, so add -er: “He arrived faster than me.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Comparative = compare two things (-er / more). Superlative = the super best of all (-est / most).
grammar.practice: Write the correct form of the adverb in brackets. | She sings ______ (loud) than her sister. | Out of all the students, he works the ______ (hard). | This book reads ______ (easy) than the last one.

grammar.title: Present Continuous for Plans
grammar.explanation: The present continuous (am/is/are + verb-ing) usually means “happening right now”. But we can also use it for plans that are already arranged for the future, especially when we say WHEN.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use: subject + am/is/are + verb-ing, and add a future time word.
“We are visiting the factories next week.”
“She is studying for her test this weekend.”
“I am meeting my friends tomorrow.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “We are plan a visit.”  ✗
Fix: Add -ing to the verb: “We are planning a visit.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of it as a “now-and-later” tense. If you can picture it happening, and you add a time word, it becomes a plan.
grammar.practice: Complete each sentence with the present continuous form. | I ______ (visit) my grandmother this weekend. | They ______ (watch) a new show tonight. | We ______ (plan) a picnic for Saturday.

grammar.title: Imperatives (Instructions)
grammar.explanation: Imperatives are verbs we use to tell someone what to do. We use them to give directions, instructions, and friendly commands — very useful when you show someone around a place!
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Start with the base verb. Do not add “you”:  “Take the elevator.” (not “You take the elevator.”)
Add please to be polite:  “Please check the map.”
For a NO, start with don't:  “Don't run in the hallway.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “Not touch that.”  ✗
Fix: Start a negative command with “don't”: “Don't touch that.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Imagine you are a friendly captain giving an order. You don't say “You go”, you just say “Go!”
grammar.practice: Fill in the blank with the correct imperative (the verb is in brackets). | ______ the book on the table. (put) | ______ your homework before dinner. (finish) | ______ the lights when you leave. (turn off)

grammar.title: Question Tags + Expressions of Emotion + Pronouns and Sentence Starters
grammar.explanation: A question tag is a short question added to the end of a sentence to check if someone agrees. It keeps a conversation friendly: “You're coming, aren't you?”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
If the sentence is positive, the tag is negative:  “You're happy, aren't you?”
If the sentence is negative, the tag is positive:  “They don't like it, do they?”
Match the pronoun:  “She can swim, can't she?”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “You're happy, are you?”  ✗
Fix: Positive sentence needs a negative tag: “You're happy, aren't you?”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of a seesaw: when the sentence goes UP (positive), the tag goes DOWN (negative), and the other way round.
grammar.practice: Choose the correct question tag. | You're coming to the party, ______? (aren't you / are you) | They don't like chocolate, ______? (do they / don't they) | She's your friend, ______? (is she / isn't she) | Lesson 7:  Expressions of Emotion | What it means and why it matters | Expressions of emotion let us share how we feel. This makes conversations warmer and helps friends understand each other. | How to form it | Use I'm + feeling:  “I'm excited!”  “I'm nervous.” | Or I feel + feeling:  “I feel hungry.” | Agree with someone by saying:  “Me too!” | A common mistake | Mistake: “I happy.”  ✗ | Fix: Always use “I'm” before the feeling: “I'm happy.”  ✓ | Memory tip | Feelings need the little word “am”. Say it as “I'm” — “I'm” + how you feel. | Practice 7 | Fill in the blank with a suitable emotion word. | I'm ______ to go to the coast! (a happy feeling) | I'm ______ because I lost my book. (a sad feeling) | My friend said she is excited. I said, “______!” (agree) | Lesson 8:  Pronouns and Sentence Starters | What it means and why it matters | Pronouns (I, you, he, she, it, we, they) take the place of names so we don't repeat them. Sentence starters like “We need…”, “Let's…”, and “I feel…” help us begin our ideas clearly. | How to form it | Replace a name with a pronoun:  “Sophia likes it.” → “She likes it.” | Begin a plan with a starter:  “Let's go to the museum.”  “We need a map.” | Use I as the doer, me as the receiver:  “I like it.”  “She gave me a book.” | A common mistake | Mistake: “Me want to go.”  ✗ | Fix: “Me” cannot be the doer. Use “I”: “I want to go.”  ✓ | Memory tip | Pronouns are shortcuts that save time. Sentence starters are keys that unlock the start of your idea. | Practice 8 | Complete each sentence using the starter or pronoun in brackets. | ______ go to the museum this weekend! (Let's) | ______ need to help each other. (We) | ______ tired after running. (I feel)

speaking.title: Speaking 1: Invite a Friend
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Use would like to to invite someone. Get ready to say:
“Would you like to visit the museum with me?”
“Would you like to come to the event in my neighbourhood?”
“I would like to try the new restaurant. Would you like to come?”
Record yourself. Check: Did you say “would like to”? Did you sound polite and friendly?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise invite a friend and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2: Tell Someone Your Plan
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Use going to to describe a plan for a trip. Say three sentences:
“On Saturday, I am going to ______.”
“We are going to arrive at the ______ at ______ o'clock.”
“After that, we are going to ______.”
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise tell someone your plan and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 3: Where Am I?
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
One person thinks of a place from the vocabulary list (station, mall, airport, museum …).
The other person asks yes/no questions: “Can you buy food there?” “Do trains stop there?”
Keep asking until you can say: “You are at the ______!” Then swap turns.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise where am i? and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 4: Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise vocabulary expert interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 5: Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain one important unit idea, give a reason with because, and respond to two follow-up questions.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise explain and respond and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 6: Three-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Plan and record a three-minute talk using an opening, three connected ideas with evidence or examples, and a purposeful closing.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise three-minute unit talk and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

writing.title: Writing 1: A Tour Plan for Your Capital City
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Imagine you are showing a visitor around your capital city. Write a short paragraph (4–5 sentences) about the tour. Include at least three places, such as a museum, a restaurant, or the airport. Use going to and arrive.
Example opening: “First, we are going to visit the museum. We will arrive at ten o'clock …”
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about a tour plan for your capital city.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 2: An Invitation to an Event
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write an invitation to an event in your neighbourhood — maybe an Eid celebration, a market day, or a football match. Use would like to at least twice. Remember to say WHAT the event is, WHERE it is, and WHEN it is.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about an invitation to an event.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 3: A Short Dialogue
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a short dialogue (6–8 lines) between a customer and a worker at a bike rental shop. Use polite language and at least one imperative (an instruction).
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about a short dialogue.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 4: Connected Paragraph
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a six-sentence paragraph about the unit. Begin with a topic sentence and join ideas with and, but or because.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about connected paragraph.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 5: Information Text
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a heading and five accurate facts. Use precise unit vocabulary and check spelling and punctuation.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about information text.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 6: Reflect and Improve
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose earlier writing, revise three sentences, add two useful details, and explain one improvement you made.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about reflect and improve.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work

activities.title: Activities 1: Match the Place to What You Do There
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each place (1–6) to the correct action (a–f).
airport
museum
restaurant
station
mall
office
a. eat a meal cooked for you
b. catch a train or a bus
c. work at a desk
d. see old things and learn about history
e. get on an aeroplane
f. shop in many stores under one roof
Answers: 1–e   2–d   3–a   4–b   5–f   6–c
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 2: Choose the Right Grammar
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write the correct choice for each sentence.
We are ______ (going to / would like) explore the capital tomorrow.
Would you ______ (like to / going to) visit the museum?
The train arrived ______ (fast / faster) than the bus.
You're coming to the event, ______ (aren't you / are you)?
______ (Take / Taking) the elevator to the fifth floor.
Answers: 1. going to   2. like to   3. faster   4. aren't you   5. Take
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 3: Fill in the Blanks (vocabulary)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Complete each sentence with a word from the unit.
The main city of a country is its ______.
A person who buys something is a ______.
The line where the sky meets the sea is the ______.
A place where aeroplanes take off and land is an ______.
The area around your home is your ______.
Answers: 1. capital   2. customer   3. horizon   4. airport   5. neighbourhood
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 4: Vocabulary Sort and Explain
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort twelve unit words into useful categories. Explain the rule for each category in a complete sentence.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 5: Reading Evidence Hunt
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Find six important details in the unit texts. Record the detail and explain what it helps the reader understand.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 6: Create and Teach
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Design a short game, diagram or demonstration that teaches one unit idea to another learner.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

assignments.title: Places, People, and Plans learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Submit Writing 1: A Tour Plan for Your Capital City and a recording of Speaking 1: Invite a Friend. Improve both pieces using the Grade 4 rubrics.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: What does 'tourism' mean?
quizzes.options: A place where trains or buses stop to pick up people. | The main city of a country, where the government is. | The track that trains travel along. | Travelling to new places for fun or to learn about them.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Travelling to new places for fun or to learn about them.
quizzes.explanation: Tourism brings many visitors to the coast every summer.

quizzes.question: What does 'airport' mean?
quizzes.options: A country and all the people who live in it. | A place where aeroplanes take off and land. | The main city of a country, where the government is. | The track that trains travel along.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A place where aeroplanes take off and land.
quizzes.explanation: We waited at the airport for our flight to Nairobi.

quizzes.question: What does 'station' mean?
quizzes.options: A building where you see old or special things and learn about them. | A country and all the people who live in it. | A place where trains or buses stop to pick up people. | The main city of a country, where the government is.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A place where trains or buses stop to pick up people.
quizzes.explanation: We waited at the station for the morning train.

quizzes.question: What does 'railroad' mean?
quizzes.options: A big shopping centre with many stores under one roof. | A building where you see old or special things and learn about them. | A country and all the people who live in it. | The track that trains travel along.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The track that trains travel along.
quizzes.explanation: The railroad runs all the way across the country.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'A Trip to the Capital': _____ brings many people to the capital, so the streets were full.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Tourism brings many people to the capital, so the streets were full.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Day We Got Lost in Mombasa': “We'll stay carefully nearby.” But the shop was full of wonderful souvenirs — little aeroplanes for the _____, capital city keychains, and even a tiny model railroad!
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “We'll stay carefully nearby.” But the shop was full of wonderful souvenirs — little aeroplanes for the airport, capital city keychains, and even a tiny model railroad!
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'A Trip to the Capital': First, we went to the big _____ and waited for the train.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: First, we went to the big station and waited for the train.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Living Near the Equator': Trains run on the _____, ships arrive at the coast, and factories make goods for the whole nation.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Trains run on the railroad, ships arrive at the coast, and factories make goods for the whole nation.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Living Near the Equator': Whether you live in a small neighbourhood or a big _____, there is always a place to go and a plan to make.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Whether you live in a small neighbourhood or a big capital, there is always a place to go and a plan to make.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'A Trip to the Capital': We visited a _____ full of old maps and tools.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: We visited a museum full of old maps and tools.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

teacherNotes.note: Model new language aloud, check meaning before independent work, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you read the listening scripts aloud and then quiz me on them?”
“Can you play a directions game where you tell me how to get somewhere in the mall?”
“Can you explain any words in the reading texts that I did not understand?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me practise inviting someone politely?”
“Can you play ‘Where Am I?’ with me and give me hints?”
“Can you listen to my plan and tell me if my ‘going to’ sentences are correct?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my tour plan and tell me what to fix?”
“Can you help me make my invitation sound more polite?”
“Can you act out my dialogue with me, playing the shop worker?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you quiz me on the ‘places’ words with a picture clue for each?”
“What is the capital of my country, and how do I say it in a full sentence?”
“Can you give me two more example sentences for the word ‘tourism’?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you play ‘Where Am I?’ and describe a place while I guess?”
“What is the difference between a mall and a museum?”
“Can you check my sentence about my neighbourhood?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you give me directions using ‘entrance’, ‘hallway’, and ‘elevator’ so I can draw them?”
“Can you check my direction sentences?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you quiz me on the words ‘customer’, ‘arrive’, ‘event’, ‘horizon’, and ‘equator’?”
“Can you help me use ‘arrive’ in three different sentences?”
“Why is it hot near the equator?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my grammar answers and explain any I got wrong?”
“Why do we say ‘aren't you?’ after ‘You're coming’?”
“Can you give me five more sentences to practise ‘going to’?”

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Travelling to new places for fun or to learn about them. Tourism brings many visitors to the coast every summer.
A place where aeroplanes take off and land. We waited at the airport for our flight to Nairobi.
A place where trains or buses stop to pick up people. We waited at the station for the morning train.
The track that trains travel along. The railroad runs all the way across the country.
The main city of a country, where the government is. Nairobi is the capital of Kenya.
A country and all the people who live in it. Kenya is a proud nation with beautiful places.
A building where you see old or special things and learn about them. We saw dinosaur bones at the museum.
A big shopping centre with many stores under one roof. We bought new shoes at the mall.
A place where people buy and eat meals that cooks make. We had rice and chicken at a new restaurant.
A place where people work at desks. My mother works in an office in the city.

selfAssessment.statement: I can talk about future plans using “going to”.

selfAssessment.statement: I can compare actions using adverbs (faster, most carefully).

selfAssessment.statement: I can give clear instructions using imperatives.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can use question tags to check what someone thinks.
